title: Listening - Part 3: Building for the Soul
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.
instructions: You will hear an interview with an architect, Elliot, and an environmental psychologist, Libby, about the impact of building design on mental health. For questions 15–20, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which fits best.

extracts.context: An interview regarding 'biophilic design,' the psychological effect of high-rise living, and the future of urban spaces.
extracts.audio_script:
Interviewer: We spend ninety percent of our time indoors. How do the walls around us shape our minds? Joining me are Elliot, an architect, and Libby, a psychologist specializing in urban environments. Elliot, what is 'neuroarchitecture'?

Elliot: [15] It’s a field that looks at how the physical environment affects our brain chemistry. For decades, we built 'functional' buildings that were essentially concrete boxes. [/15] We now know that sharp angles and low ceilings increase cortisol—the stress hormone. As architects, we are starting to use more curves and natural light to create spaces that actually lower blood pressure and improve focus.

Interviewer: Libby, you’ve studied the impact of high-rise living. Are we meant to live in the clouds?

Libby: [16] Humans evolved in the savannah, with wide-open views and a connection to the horizon. When you live on the fortieth floor of a tower, you lose that 'grounding.' [/16] Interestingly, people in high-rises often feel more isolated because they lack the 'accidental' social interactions you get on a street level. We are seeing higher rates of anxiety in people who live in environments that feel disconnected from nature.

Interviewer: Elliot, 'biophilic design' is a big trend right now. Is it just about adding a few plants to the office?

Elliot: [17] It’s much deeper than that. It’s about integrating 'fractal' patterns—the repeating shapes we see in trees or clouds—into the building's structure. [/17] Our brains find these patterns inherently soothing. It’s also about 'thermal variability'—allowing for natural air movement rather than a constant, artificial temperature. It makes the building feel like a living organism rather than a machine.

Interviewer: Libby, what about the 'open-plan' office? Is it the disaster everyone says it is?

Libby: [18] For most people, yes. While it was designed to encourage collaboration, it actually does the opposite. People wear headphones to create a 'digital wall' because they lack privacy and control. [/18] A healthy workspace needs 'prospect and refuge'—the ability to see what’s going on but also a safe, enclosed space where you feel protected. Without that, the brain stays in a state of low-level 'hyper-vigilance.'

Interviewer: Elliot, is sustainable architecture also better for the mind?

Elliot: [19] Absolutely. Using natural materials like wood or stone instead of plastic and steel has a measurable psychological benefit. Wood, in particular, has a 'warmth' that people respond to emotionally. [/19] Building sustainably isn't just about the planet; it’s about the people inside.

Interviewer: Final thoughts, Libby? How can people improve their own homes?

Libby: [20] Small changes matter. Maximize natural light, bring in greenery, and pay attention to 'acoustics.' Noise is a major, often invisible, stressor. If you can create a quiet 'sanctuary' in your home, your brain will thank you. [/20]

Interviewer: Elliot, Libby, thank you.

questions.question: Elliot says that traditional 'concrete box' buildings can cause
options: A) a decrease in brain activity.
B) higher levels of a hormone related to stress.
C) people to become more creative and focused.
D) a physical increase in heart rate and blood pressure.
questions.explanation: He mentions that sharp angles and low ceilings in such buildings increase cortisol, which is the stress hormone.

questions.question: According to Libby, why is high-rise living problematic for humans?
options: A) It is too far for people to walk to the shops.
B) It disconnects us from our natural environmental evolution.
C) The air quality is much worse on higher floors.
D) It makes people more likely to talk to their neighbors.
questions.explanation: She explains that humans evolved in the savannah and high-rises make us lose that 'grounding' and connection to the horizon.

questions.question: What is a key feature of 'biophilic design' according to Elliot?
options: A) using bright, neon colors to stimulate the brain
B) incorporating patterns that mimic those found in nature
C) keeping the temperature of a room exactly the same all day
D) avoiding the use of windows to keep out noise.
questions.explanation: He mentions integrating 'fractal patterns'—repeating shapes seen in trees or clouds—to soothe the brain.

questions.question: Libby criticizes open-plan offices because they
options: A) are too expensive for most companies to build.
B) prevent employees from using their computers correctly.
C) fail to provide the sense of privacy that workers need.
D) encourage people to talk too much instead of working.
questions.explanation: She argues that workers lack privacy and control, leading them to use 'digital walls' (headphones) to escape.

questions.question: Elliot believes that using wood in buildings is beneficial because
options: A) it is more durable than plastic or steel.
B) it creates an emotional sense of warmth in people.
C) it is the only material that is truly sustainable.
D) it makes the building look much more expensive.
questions.explanation: He states that wood has a 'warmth' that people respond to emotionally, providing a psychological benefit.

questions.question: Libby's final recommendation is for people to
options: A) move to a house with more rooms.
B) spend more money on high-end furniture.
C) focus on reducing noise and increasing natural light.
D) stop working from home altogether.
questions.explanation: She advises maximizing natural light, adding greenery, and paying attention to 'acoustics' (noise reduction).
